Document Title: MMDA: A Multimodal and Multisource Domain Adaptation Method for Cross-Subject Emotion Recognition From EEG and Eye Movement Signals
Authors: M. Jiménez-Guarneros; G. Fuentes-Pineda; J. Grande-Barreto
Author Affiliations: Department of Computer Science, Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas (IIMAS), Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México (UNAM), Coyoacán, Ciudad de México, Mexico; Department of Computer Science, Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas (IIMAS), Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México (UNAM), Coyoacán, Ciudad de México, Mexico; Information Technology Engineering, Universidad Politécnica de Puebla, Cuanalá, Puebla, Mexico
Publication Title: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
Abstract: Multimodal emotion recognition from electroencephalogram (EEG) and eye movement signals has shown to be a promising approach to provide more discriminative information about human emotional states. However, most current works rely on a subject-dependent approach, limiting their applicability to new users. Recently, some studies have explored multimodal domain adaptation to address the mentioned issue by transferring information from known subjects to new ones. Unfortunately, existing methods are still exposed to negative transfer as a suboptimal distribution alignment is performed between subjects, while irrelevant information is not discarded. In this article, we present a multimodal and multisource domain adaptation (MMDA) method, which adopts the following three strategies: 1) marginal and conditional distribution alignments must be performed between each known subject and a new one; 2) relevant distribution alignments must be prioritized to avoid a negative transfer; and 3) modality fusion results should be improved by extracting more discriminative features from EEG signals and selecting relevant features across modalities. Our proposed method was evaluated with leave-one-subject-out cross validation on four public datasets: SEED, SEED-GER, SEED-IV, and SEED-V. Experimental results show that our proposal outperforms state-of-the-art results for each dataset when subject data from different sessions are combined into a single dataset. Moreover, MMDA exceeds the state of the art in 8 out of 11 different sessions when each session is evaluated.
Author Keywords: Deep learning;electroencephalogram (EEG);eye movement (EM);multimodal emotion recognition (MER);multimodal unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA);multisource domain adaptation (MDA)
IEEE Terms: Electroencephalography;Emotion recognition;Neural networks;Feature extraction;Data models;Training;Physiology;Correlation;Brain modeling;Adaptation models

Document Title: Fault diagnosis in a HVAC monophase transmission line
Authors: E. A. G. Rodriguez; C. Verde
Author Affiliations: Instituto de Ingenieria Universidad Nacional Autonoma de Mexico Delegación Coyoacán, Ciudad de México; Instituto de Ingenieria Universidad Nacional Autonoma de Mexico Delegación Coyoacán, Ciudad de México
Publication Title: 2025 International Conference on Control, Automation and Diagnosis (ICCAD)
Abstract: This paper applies linear attention methods (LAMs) for creating models that recognize voltage sag, swell and harmonics in high voltage alternate current (HVAC) for a monophase transmission line. The method can simultaneously predict all the faults while working with various windows frames even as small as a half cycle. The approach is based on using data and allowing the model to learn the mathematical relationship between the input and the expected output by itself. The usage of LAMs made effective and precise predictions, in addition to the faster processing of information as compared with traditional attention models. The inclusion of different types of noises has not greatly affected the model’s performance, which produces a reliable method for identifying these types of distortions.
IEEE Terms: Fault diagnosis;Adaptation models;Power transmission lines;HVAC;Noise;Distortion;Transmission line measurements;Mathematical models;Data models;Distortion measurement

Document Title: Fast Taylor-Fourier Transform for Monitoring Modern Power Grids With Real-Time Dynamic Harmonic Estimation
Authors: J. A. d. l. O. Serna; A. Zamora-Mendez; G. E. Mejia-Ruiz; M. R. A. Paternina; D. Dotta; L. Lugnani; M. Garcia; P. R. D. R. Silva
Author Affiliations: Department of Electrical Engineering, Autonomous University of Nuevo Leon, Monterrey, NL, Mexico; Electrical Engineering Faculty, State University of Michoacan, Morelia, Mexico; Electrical Engineering-Power Systems, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico, Mexico; Department of Electrical Engineering, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico, Mexico; Department of Electrical Engineering, University of Campinas, Campinas, Brazil; Department of Electrical Engineering, University of Campinas, Campinas, Brazil; Department of Electrical Engineering, University of Campinas, Campinas, Brazil; Department of Electrical Engineering, University of Campinas, Campinas, Brazil
Abstract: This paper proposes the FTFT for the real-time monitoring of modern power grids through dynamic harmonic estimations. The FTFT results from the DTTFT implemented with the FFT, instead of its filter bank implementation. In FTFT, the harmonic Taylor-Fourier coefficients are obtained in batch, and from these complex coefficients, estimates of frequency, amplitude, and RoCoF are obtained. The FTFT is embedded in two low-cost platforms, one consisting of a digital signal processor (DSP) coded using C programming language, and a development PMU (D-PMU); where a computationally efficient calculation of the FTFT is coded in both platforms. Both platforms process instantaneous voltage and current signals, rendering phasor and harmonic estimates as if they were extracted by the impulse response of a set of FIR filters and their first and second differentiators of the DTTFT. The phasor and harmonic estimations are accomplished using a sliding window that updates sample by sample, instantaneously providing amplitude, phase, frequency, and RoCoF estimations at the fundamental and harmonic frequencies with a high reporting rate. Experimental results confirm the proposition's performance, precision, and effectiveness under steady-state conditions, sudden changes, and harmonic analysis.

Authors: F. N. Murrieta-Rico; O. Sergiyenko; P. Mercorelli; V. Petranovskii
Author Affiliations: Ingeniería Mecatrónica, Universidad Politécnica de Baja California, Mexicali, México; Instituto de Ingeniería, Universidad Autónoma de Baja California, Mexicali, México; Leuphana University of Lueneburg, Lueneburg, Germany; Centro de Nanociencias y Nanotecnología, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Ensenada, México
Publication Title: 2025 Joint Conference of the European Frequency and Time Forum and IEEE International Frequency Control Symposium (EFTF/IFCS)
Abstract: SummaryThe estimation of frequency in a signal is a task that is required by plenty of devices, where the knowledge of the frequency can lead to retrieving important information. Accordingly, a good approximation to the desired frequency is required. Although there are different methods, principles and devices for accomplishing such a task, in practice, fast and accurate ways for estimating frequency are always sought. In recent years, the method of rational approximations has been explored as a novel tool for estimating frequency, where its main leverage over other methods is that the time where uncertainty reduces is decreased as a consequence of an increment of the desired and reference frequency values. For this method, although different applications have been found and many characteristics about how it works have been studied, there are aspects that need to be further explored. In particular, this work addresses how the mediant fractions appear in an experimental measurement process.
IEEE Terms: Time-frequency analysis;Uncertainty;Accuracy;Atmospheric measurements;Process control;Europe;Estimation;Particle measurements;Frequency estimation;Frequency control

Abstract: This study explores the mechanical and chemical properties of Luffa aegyptiaca fiber reinforced epoxy composites, enhanced through various chemical treatments. Luffa aegyptiaca fibers, both in particulate and short forms, were subjected to treatments using NaOH, benzoyl chloride, and phenyltriethoxysilane to modify their surface chemistry and topography, as evidenced by Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) and Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) analyses. The mechanical properties, including tensile and flexural strength, alongside water absorption capabilities and chemical resistance, were evaluated for both treated and untreated fibers. Results demonstrated significant improvements in mechanical strengths and chemical resistance in treated fibers compared to their untreated counterparts, with saline treated particulate fiber composites showing the most substantial enhancements. These findings suggest that treated Luffa aegyptiaca fiber composites could serve as viable materials for applications such as furniture and construction components, providing a sustainable alternative to conventional synthetic composites.

Document Title: Exploratory Data Analysis and Classification of Hand Electromyographic Signals Using Classical Machine Learning Algorithms with Hyperparameters Optimization
Authors: J. L. Garza Rivera; A. E. Godínez; D. T. Varela
Author Affiliations: Universidad Da Vinci, CDMX, México; Universidad Da Vinci, CDMX, México; Facultad de Estudios Superiores Cuautitlán Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México Cuautitlan, Izcalli, México
Publication Title: 2025 IEEE Latin American School on Computational Intelligence and Robotics (SAIBOT)
Abstract: Digital signal processing involves recording a signal, making changes to it, and generating a new signal. In engineering, procedures have been developed to acquire signals and extract useful data from them. Once the characteristics have been extracted, they are classified using statistical and artificial intelligence methods. Understanding, comparing and using these characteristics is an important step before tackling massive data sets and deep learning algorithms. The limited capabilities of the embedded cards that can be included in EMG prostheses must also be considered. This paper proposes a basic exploratory analysis and the use of classical algorithms to decompose a set of EMG data. Various machine learning classification algorithms were evaluated, including multilayer perceptron neural networks, support vector machines (SVM), K-nearest neighbors (KNN), decision trees and random forests. The results were then compared using the accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and support value metrics. Classic machine learning algorithms are still an option for programming embedded systems that operate based on EMG signals. The quality and cleanliness of the data are as important as the choice of AI model.
Author Keywords: Classification algorithms;Data analysis;Feature extraction;Digital signal processing;Machine learning;Electromyography
IEEE Terms: Measurement;Support vector machines;Machine learning algorithms;Data analysis;Signal processing algorithms;Programming;Nearest neighbor methods;Electromyography;Data models;Classification algorithms

Document Title: Spectral Characterization of the Ictal Phase in Epileptic Seizures of Temporal Lobe Using the Wavelet Transform
Authors: G. González-González; V. M. Velasco-Herrera; C. Alvarado-Serrano; A. Ortega-Aguilar
Author Affiliations: Biophysics and Medical Physics Department, Faculty of Sciences, UNAM, Mexico City, Mexico; Solar Radiation Department, Institute of Geophysics, UNAM, Mexico City, Mexico; Electrical Engineering Department, Bioelectronics Section, CINVESTAV, Mexico City, Mexico; Biochemistry Department, Faculty of Medicine, UNAM, Mexico City, Mexico
Publication Title: 2025 22nd International Conference on Electrical Engineering, Computing Science and Automatic Control (CCE)
Abstract: Epilepsy is a chronic disorder of brain function related to neuronal hyperactivity and hypersychronization. The electroencephalogram is the main clinical technic used to the epilepsy diagnosis and study. The ictal phase of the epileptic activity occurs when the brain and behavior manifestations are most prominent, however, there are no objective parameters to determine it, as well as to identify its onset and offset. In this research we propose a spectral time-frequency analysis to characterize this phase. Twenty-one ictal phases of temporal lobe epilepsy from men and women with focal onset were analyzed with the wavelet transform. Specific power and frequency patterns on the spectrograms were identified in 75% of ictal phases: an intermittent activity in $\delta$ band, followed by a hypersynchronization in $\delta$ and $\theta$ bands, and finally a power dispersion from 2 to 30 Hz; regardless of onset lateralization and sex. This pattern clearly limited the onset and offset of the seizure. Additionally, power and frequencies dispersion analysis between the nearest channels to the focus and the other channels were performed. No dispersion patterns were observed, but it was proposed that it could be related to the individual brain damage caused by the recurrent seizures. In conclusion, the quantitative analysis of ictal phases by means of wavelet transform offered a new visualization of the ictal phase and highlighted objective information in power and frequency, which can be used for helping with understanding and classification of this kind of seizures.

Document Title: Power System Harmonics Identification Powered by an Eigensystem Realization Approach
Authors: M. G. Juarez; F. Reyes-Maldonado; A. Zamora-Mendez; J. Ortiz-Bejar; J. C. Silva-Chavez; M. R. Arrieta Paternina; V. Torres-Garcia
Author Affiliations: Michoacan University of Saint Nicholas of Hidalgo (UMSNH), MX; Michoacan University of Saint Nicholas of Hidalgo (UMSNH), MX; Universidad Michoacana San Nicolas de Hidalgo, MX; Michoacan University of Saint Nicholas of Hidalgo (UMSNH), MX; Michoacan University of Saint Nicholas of Hidalgo (UMSNH), MX; National Autonomous University of Mexico (UNAM), MX; Instituto Tecnologico de Morelia, MX
Abstract: The proliferation of power electronic devices through the massive integration of renewable energy sources on the small and medium scales in power systems has unleashed high harmonic distortions visible in electrical variables such as voltages and currents. Besides the deteriorating power quality, this fact also imposes challenges in quantifying power quality indicators, advocating novel approaches that deal with this issue. In this context, this paper counteracts such challenges by proposing a strategy to effectively measure the individual and total harmonic distortions in modern power grids. The proposal conceives the well-known system identification technique, the eigensystem realization algorithm, as a filter bank to extract the harmonic components from actual measurements and simulated data. Simulation results of two test systems confirm the effectiveness of the proposed method in attaining reliable estimates for the power system transient harmonics, inter-harmonics, and sub-harmonics. Furthermore, actual measurements corresponding to the energizing of a bank of three single-phase transformers connected in grounded Wye-Delta in an isolated lab-scale system are used. The well-known fast Fourier transform (FFT) approach is also applied to validate the proposition, resulting in the proposed ERA-based method is up to 50 times more precise in some cases than FFT.
Author Keywords: eigensystem realization algorithm;system identification;filter bank frequency response;power quality;total harmonic distortion
IEEE Terms: Power harmonic filters;Harmonic analysis;Filter banks;Finite impulse response filters;Monitoring;Fast Fourier transforms;Proposals;Power quality;Interference;Estimation

Publication Title: 2024 21st International Conference on Electrical Engineering, Computing Science and Automatic Control (CCE)
Abstract: This study evaluates the adsorption efficiency of dyes (methylene blue, malachite green, and Rhodamine B) on charcoal and activated charcoal under controlled conditions. Significant variations in adsorption were observed which can be attributed to the molecular structure of the dye and the surface properties of the adsorbent. Malachite green showed the highest adsorption on inactivated charcoal, followed by methylene blue and Rhodamine B. Activation of charcoal increased the adsorption capacity for all dyes, especially for methylene blue and Rhodamine B, due to the introduction of oxygenated functional groups. Kinetic studies showed that the adsorption conformed to the pseudo-second-order model. The results suggest that activated charcoal is a promising material for dye removal, especially for those with lower initial affinities.

Document Title: Quasi-PT Symmetry: A Pathway to Better Laser Performance
Authors: B. Olyaeefar; E. Karabey; E. Şeker; R. El-Ganainy; A. Demir
Author Affiliations: UNAM - Institute of Materials Science and Nanotechnology, Bilkent University, Ankara, Turkey; UNAM - Institute of Materials Science and Nanotechnology, Bilkent University, Ankara, Turkey; UNAM - Institute of Materials Science and Nanotechnology, Bilkent University, Ankara, Turkey; Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Saint Louis University, Saint Louis, MO, USA; UNAM - Institute of Materials Science and Nanotechnology, Bilkent University, Ankara, Turkey
Publication Title: 2025 Conference on Lasers and Electro-Optics Europe & European Quantum Electronics Conference (CLEO/Europe-EQEC)
Abstract: Semiconductor laser diodes (LDs) are key enablers in various applications, including optical communication, material processing, and healthcare, because of their compact size, efficiency, and ease of integration. However, increasing LD output power while preserving spatial and spectral beam quality remains a challenge, often limited by nonlinear effects and filamentation. Established solutions, such as photonic crystals in VCSELs or tapered/antiguiding designs in EELDs, boost power and beam quality but inflate fabrication complexity and cost, hindering widespread adoption. [1]. Recently, non-Hermitian photonics has emerged as a paradigm for engineering the spatial distribution of optical gain and loss. Parity-time-symmetric (PTS) lasers, in particular, can achieve single-mode operation in both longitudinal and transverse directions. More advanced approaches, combining non-Hermitian engineering, supersymmetry, and topological physics, have been proposed for large-area laser arrays, yet realistic power levels and high beam quality remain elusive, often relying on pulsed pumping or exhibiting spurious passive-waveguide emission [2], [3]. Here, we present a systematic theoretical and experimental comparison of PTS and quasi-PTS (q-PTS) EELDs (see Fig. 1). Although both architectures achieve comparable output powers under similar pumping conditions, only q-PTS lasers maintain excellent beam quality (M2 < 1.5) at power levels near 0.5 W, surpassing PTS devices (M2 ≈ 4). Moreover, power conversion efficiency remains unaffected in q-PTS structures, indicating a minimal trade-off between filtering higher-order modes and preserving overall device efficiency. Equally important, these benefits are achieved without adding complexity or additional fabrication steps beyond a conventional EELD process. These findings challenge the prevailing notion that spatial filtering must be enforced by PTS, which can introduce side lobes that degrade beam quality. Instead, q-PTS leverages stronger coupling among higher-order modes while leaving the fundamental mode unperturbed, striking an optimal balance between mode selectivity and practicality. Our results, therefore, offer a roadmap for large-area single-mode EELDs that deliver both high power and high beam quality, translating non-Hermitian concepts from proof-of-concept to real-world impact.
IEEE Terms: Laser theory;Filtering;Power lasers;Laser modes;Laser excitation;Complexity theory;Laser beams;Vertical cavity surface emitting lasers;Pump lasers;Power generation

Document Title: A Real Time Strategy for Detecting Intertum Short-Circuit Faults in PMSM Using Vold-Kalman Filter
Authors: M. A. Mazzoletti; O. M. Simonovets; G. R. Bossio; F. R. Gentile
Author Affiliations: UDEE-F1, UNaM, Oberá, Argentina; UDEE-F1, UNaM-CONICET, Oberá, Argentina; GEA-IITEMA, UNRC-CONICET, Río Cuarto, Argentina; FI-UBA, Instituto de Ingenierta Biomedica, Buenos Aires, Argentina
Publication Title: 2025 XXI Workshop on Information Procesing and Control (RPIC)
Abstract: This paper presents a strategy for detecting interturn short-circuit (ITSC) faults in permanent magnet synchronous machines (PMSM) based on tracking the fundamental component of a residual voltage using the Vold-Kalman filter (VKF). The vector models of the voltages at the midpoint of the phase windings for a faulty PMSM are presented. In addition, the orthogonal vector (OV) model of the VKF algorithm used to compute the magnitude and phase shift from the measured voltage is described. Experimental results for a PMSM with ITSC of varying severity under both no-load and full-load conditions are presented. It is demonstrated that a ITSC fault can be reliably detected from 1 % fault severity, and the corresponding faulty winding can be accurately identified.

Abstract: In this work, we investigated unprecedented laser-induced rapid synthesis (LIRS) cobalt-based metal-organic framework (Co-MOF) saturable absorber (SA) to generate a conventional soliton pulse in a thulium-doped fiber laser (TDFL). The Co-MOF was drop-casted onto a side-polished fiber (SPF) to prepare the saturable absorber (SA). It exhibited a high modulation depth of 14% and a saturation intensity of 20.7MW/cm2. The pulse produced had a pulse width and center wavelength of 1.34 ps and 1974.2 nm, respectively. The repetition rate of the output was 13.09 MHz, which gives a pulse interval of about 76.4 ps. The beam output reached an average power of 6.56 mW and pulse energy of about 501 pJ before breaking, achieving a maximum peak power of 374 W.

Authors: E. De Armas; G. Díaz; I. Sobron; J. M. Matías; I. Eizmendi
Author Affiliations: UNAM: Dept. Telecommunications, Mexico City, Mexico; UPV/EHU: Dept. Communication Engineering, Bilbao, Spain
Publication Title: 2025 IEEE 21st International Conference on Factory Communication Systems (WFCS)
Abstract: The fields of human presence detection (HPD) and people counting (PC) from WiFi-Channel State Information (CSI) represent constantly evolving areas of research within the scientific community. This study aims to expand the research frontiers by exploring the application of HPD and PC in an industrial environment characterized by its considerable dimensions, the presence of obstacles and machines, and harsh propagation channel conditions. Using a convolutional neural network (CNN) incorporating CSI measurements, we demonstrated the ability to distinguish between human presence and industrial machine activity with the same model, achieving an accuracy of over 99%. In addition, a study was conducted to count up to three people, even when machines were on, with an accuracy of over 92%. Another experiment was conducted with the objective of assessing not only the feasibility of PC in a naturally challenging environment but also the impact of the WiFi channel bandwidth in this particular application. The results of this study highlight the potential of WiFi-CSI sensing to address a significant challenge in human activity detection and estimation: its application in industrial environments.

Publication Title: 2025 IEEE International Autumn Meeting on Power, Electronics and Computing (ROPEC)
Abstract: This paper addresses power system coherency by extracting oscillatory modal characteristics, which in turn inform a hierarchical agglomerative clustering (HAC) technique. The phase of the slower frequency modes is extracted from rotor speed measurements after a large disturbance occurs using a set of FIR filters built with the discrete-time Taylor-Fourier transform (DTTFT). These are clustered using the hierarchical agglomerative clustering (HAC) technique, with a subsequent optimal determination of the number of clusters guided by a customized Elbow heuristic, constructed explicitly over the HAC cluster merging distance/cost curve. Finally, the attained results demonstrate that the DTTFT’s noise tolerance under polluted conditions and the effectiveness of the HAC for properly clustering generating units in the 10-machine and 39-bus benchmark power grid.

Document Title: Measurement and Analysis of the Inrush Current in a 60 MVA Power Transformer
Authors: M. Vazquez; V. Torres-García; L. N. Rueda-Can; N. Solís-Ramos; S. Ramirez-Zavala
Author Affiliations: Funken/UNAM; TecNM/Instituto Tecnológico de Morelia; TecNM/Instituto Tecnológico de Morelia; CFE, Comisión Federal de Electricidad; Universidad Michoacana de San Nicolás de Hidalgo
Publication Title: 2024 IEEE PES Generation, Transmission and Distribution Latin America Conference and Industrial Exposition (GTDLA)
Abstract: Electrical power transformers play a crucial role in the electrical power system, as they are responsible for increasing voltage levels to transmit electrical energy from generating plants and subsequently reducing them to the required levels for consumers. However, when a transformer is energized, a transient phenomenon known as Inrush current occurs due to the saturation of the core. A key characteristic of this phenomenon is that the current can reach a magnitude several times greater than the transformer’s nominal current, which may cause false trips in the breakers. Also, The current waveform associated with this phenomenon is asymmetric, dampens over time, and is predominantly composed of the second harmonic component. This work analyzes the harmonic current content observed during the energization of a real 60 MVA transformer. The recorded currents of two cases were analyzed using the Discrete Fourier Transform DFT, showing that the second harmonic is representative of this type of phenomenon.
IEEE Terms: Discrete Fourier transforms;Surge protection;Power system harmonics;Transformer cores;Harmonic analysis;Power transformers;Circuit faults;Transient analysis;Protection;Inrush current

Author Affiliations: School of Machinery and Transportation, Changzhou Vocational Institute of Industry Technology, Changzhou, China; School of Machinery and Transportation, Changzhou Vocational Institute of Industry Technology, Changzhou, China; Institute of Acoustics, School of Physics Science and Engineering, Tongji University, Shanghai, China; State Key Laboratory of Dynamic Testing Technology, North University of China, Taiyuan, China; State Key Laboratory of Dynamic Testing Technology, North University of China, Taiyuan, China; State Key Laboratory of Dynamic Testing Technology, North University of China, Taiyuan, China; National Nanotechnology Research Center (UNAM), Bilkent University, Ankara, Turkey; Institute of Ultrasonic Testing, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, China
Abstract: It is widely recognized that the use of multimode ultrasonic guided waves holds the potential for a comprehensive evaluation of structural damages, including their locations, sizes, and types, in the realm of damage imaging. Recently, a micro-electromechanical system (MEMS) sensor, named 3-D capacitive micromachined ultrasonic transducer (3D-CMUT), has emerged as a promising candidate for detecting 3-D vibrations of multimode ultrasonic guided waves. In this work, 3D-CMUT is introduced for the first time in ultrasonic guided wave-based damage imaging. Initially, for analytical convenience, an idealized design of 3D-CMUT is substituted with a simplified configuration comprising a piston-type cell (sensing z-directional signals) and two orthogonally positioned identical comb-type cells (sensing x- and y-directional signals). Subsequently, finite element method (FEM) simulations are employed to establish models of “a damaged aluminum plate with a circular through-hole” and “an undamaged aluminum plate.” By using the transfer function-based effective method, 3-D scattered signals captured by an array of 3D-CMUTs are rapidly extracted to characterize the circular through-hole. Ultimately, the scattered data are transformed into intuitive damage images using the decomposition of the time-reversal operator and multiple signal classification (DORT-MUSIC) algorithm. The results not only affirm the viability of applying 3D-CMUT in damage imaging but also underscore the advantages of integrating 3-D ultrasonic guided wave information for a more accurate assessment of structural damages. This research may contribute significantly to advancing the applications of 3D-CMUT in nondestructive testing (NDT).

Author Keywords: 3-D capacitive micromachined ultrasonic transducers (3D-CMUTs);damage imaging;ultrasonic guided waves

Document Title: Oscillation Source Location Assisted by the O-splines Filter
Authors: R. Dominguez-Navarrete; A. Zamora; J. Ortiz-Bejar; M. R. A. Paternina
Author Affiliations: Universidad Michoacana de San Nicolás de Hidalgo, Morelia, Mexico; Universidad Michoacana de San Nicolás de Hidalgo, Morelia, Mexico; Universidad Michoacana de San Nicolás de Hidalgo, Morelia, Mexico; UNAM, Mexico City
Publication Title: 2025 IEEE International Autumn Meeting on Power, Electronics and Computing (ROPEC)
Abstract: In this paper, an efficient filtering process is proposed to improve the location of oscillation sources in power systems. The proposed method is based on the multiresolution discrete-time Taylor–Fourier transform (DTTFT) designed with O-splines. This multiresolution approach employs a specific design of bandpass filters for the precise extraction of oscillatory components from measured synchrophasor signals. This allows the integration of the Taylor-Fourier filter-bank with O-splines into the well-known dissipating energy flow (DEF) method for oscillation source location, which conventionally uses the Butterworth bandpass filter. The proposed O-splines filter, combined with the DEF method, has been validated considering different scenarios of forced oscillations, where four test cases were created from the well-known Kundur two-area power system to validate the proposal. The attained results confirm that the proposal’s advantages compared with the Butterworth filter, since it exhibits an outstanding oscillation source location performance, especially at the beginning of the disturbance.
IEEE Terms: Band-pass filters;Filtering;Energy resolution;Position measurement;Power electronics;Phasor measurement units;Power systems;Proposals;Oscillators;Signal resolution

Document Title: Adsorption of Malachite Green on CaO-ZnFe2O4 Composite Derived from Agro-industrial Waste
Authors: O. Reyes-Vallejo; R. M. Sánchez-Albores; R. Escobar-Velázquez; C. López-Aguilar; F. J. Cano; A. Ashok; J. Escorcia-García; P. Bartolo-Pérez
Author Affiliations: Sección de Electrónica de Estado Sólido- Ingeniería eléctrica (SEES), CINVESTAV-IPN, Ciudad de México, México; Escuela de Ciencias Químicas, Universidad Autónoma de Chiapas (UNACH), Chiapas, México; Escuela de Ciencias Químicas, Universidad Autónoma de Chiapas (UNACH), Chiapas, México; Escuela de Ciencias Químicas, Universidad Autónoma de Chiapas (UNACH), Chiapas, México; Institute of Molecules & Materials of Le Mans (IMMM) - Le Mans, France Programa de Nanociencias y Nanotecnología, CINVESTAV-IPN, Ciudad de México, México; Departamento de materia condensada, Instituto de Física, UNAM, Ciudad de México, México; CINVESTAV-IPN Unidad, Saltillo, Coahuila, México; CINVESTAV-IPN Unidad, Merida, Yucatán, México
Publication Title: 2024 21st International Conference on Electrical Engineering, Computing Science and Automatic Control (CCE)
Abstract: This study focuses on the synthesis of a CaO-ZnFe2O4 composite derived from waste materials such as eggshell CaO and ZnFe2O4 via a combustion process using orange peel as fuel. The composite was prepared by mechanical milling, sonication in ethanol, and annealing. Structural, morphological, and surface characterizations were carried out using X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and Brunauer-Emmett- Teller (BET) analysis, which confirmed the successful formation of the composite. The material was shown to be highly effective in removing malachite green dye through adsorption studies, demonstrating a significant capacity for dye removal. Kinetic modeling based on the pseudo-second-order equation indicated that the adsorption process was driven by chemisorption. In addition, the adsorption process was found to be endothermic, as the removal of malachite green increased with increasing solution temperature.
IEEE Terms: Scanning electron microscopy;Waste materials;X-ray scattering;Adsorption;Green products;Milling;X-ray diffraction;Mathematical models;Kinetic theory;Surface treatment
